description_blurb:
This document is a Mission Report (MISREP), a standardized reporting form the U.S. Military uses to record the circumstances surrounding its operations. U.S. military services often use MISREPs to report Unidentified Anomalous Phenomena (UAP) to AARO. The GENTEXT, or “general text” section of these reports often contains important qualitative, contextual information, distinguishing it from the more quantitative, or numerical, data found elsewhere in the report.
A U.S. military operator reported observing a UAP “flying just above the surface of the ocean.” The report describes the UAP as “[flying] straight above the ocean towards lands.”
All descriptive and estimative language contained in this report reflects the reporter’s subjective interpretation at the time of the event. Such characterizations should not be interpreted as a conclusive indication of the presence or absence of any intrinsic object features or performance characteristics.
title: DOW-UAP-D35, Mission Report, Greece, October 2023
summary: A U.S. military operator conducting an ISR mission observed a seemingly circular UAP flying just above the surface of the ocean water towards land. The UAP was lost from the observation feed shortly after observation. The observer assessed the UAP as benign and not under intelligent control.
reported_object_description: SEEMINGLY CIRCULAR, TOO SMALL TO MAKE OUT DETAILS. Physical State: Solid.
reported_behavior: Flying just above the surface of the ocean water, flew straight above the ocean towards lands. No observed maneuverability or response to observer actions. Propulsion means unknown.
official_resolution: Observer assessed UAP as benign and not under intelligent control.
bust_reasoning: The bust score is high due to multiple factors pointing towards a mundane explanation. The object's description is vague ('seemingly circular, too small to make out details'), its behavior is entirely prosaic ('flew straight,' 'no observed maneuverability'), and the observer explicitly assessed 'NO' intelligent control and 'NONE' for UAP signatures. The estimated 30 MPH velocity is also consistent with many known small objects. While multiple sensors (FMV, SIGINT, G-MESH) were available for the mission, the report does not indicate convergent detection or anomalous returns from SIGINT or G-MESH for the UAP, suggesting it was primarily an FMV observation that was 'lost from their feed' without further interrogation.

# DOW-UAP-D35, Mission Report, Greece, October 2023 - Page 1

Declassified by MG Richard A. Harrison
USCENTCOM Chief of Staff
Declassified on: 22 January 2026

[redacted]

# Misrep 9337873

## Narrative

[redacted]
AT 1504Z, [1.4a] TOOK OFF FROM LGLR. AT 1515Z, [1.4a] HANDED OVER FROM THE LRE. [1.4a] PROCEEDED TO FRAGGED TASKING TO SUPPORT DATA MASKED.

AT 1618Z, [1.4a] WAS 7-LINED TO SUPPORT DATA MASKED. [1.4a] ARRIVED ON-STATION AT 2018Z, CHECKED IN WITH DATA MASKED, AND PERFORMED FMV/SIGINT COLLECTION AT 36S YC 40[1.4a] 57[1.4a] (SEE ISR 1).

AT 0811Z, [1.4a] OBSERVED 1X POSS UAP (SEE UAP 1).

AT 0542Z, [1.4a] RETURNED TO BASE. AT 1035Z, [1.4a] HANDED BACK TO THE LRE. AT 1105Z, [1.4a] LANDED AT LGLR. FULL MOTION VIDEO WAS EXPLOITED BY GET. 20:01 MISSION HOURS, 09:24 FMV HOURS, 1X FMV TASKINGS PROSECUTED.
[redacted]

([redacted]) Mission Narrative

cover_up_reasoning: The document exhibits substantial concealment through extensive redactions. While the document is declassified, numerous critical details such as the original classification level, associated caveats, specific aircraft callsigns and tail numbers, precise geographic coordinates of the UAP sighting, and the full names and contact information of personnel involved are withheld. Significant portions of the mission narrative and UAP event description are also redacted. This level of redaction goes beyond routine PII protection and obscures key operational context and specific details of the observation.
claims: A UAP was observed by a U.S. military operator during an ISR mission.
The UAP was described as seemingly circular and too small to make out details.
The UAP was flying just above the surface of the ocean water.
The UAP flew straight above the ocean towards land.
The UAP was lost from the observation feed.
The observer assessed the UAP as benign and not under intelligent control.
bust_explanations: Small drone/UAS. The object is described as 'seemingly circular, too small to make out details,' flying 'just above the surface of the ocean water' at an estimated 30 MPH. Crucially, the report explicitly states 'UAP Under Intelligent Control: NO' and 'UAP Maneuverability Observations: NONE,' which are highly consistent with a small, non-anomalous drone, possibly on a pre-programmed flight path or drifting.

Weather balloon or similar low-altitude atmospheric object. A small balloon, especially if partially deflated or at a distance, could appear circular and fly straight. While 'just above the surface' is less typical for a fully buoyant balloon, it's plausible for a low-flying or descending object.

Bird or large marine animal. Given the 'too small to make out details' description, the estimated 30 MPH speed, and the low altitude 'just above the surface of the ocean water,' a misidentified bird or even a marine animal (like a large fish breaching) could be considered, though 'flying' implies airborne.
cover_up_indicators: Redaction of original classification and associated caveats
Extensive redaction of personnel names, units, and contact information (POC, QC, Approver)
Redaction of specific aircraft callsigns, tail numbers, and asset types
Redaction of precise geographic coordinates for the UAP observation
Sentence-level black-bar redaction within the mission narrative and UAP event description
